symbols: arrows piercing the body — the instruments of martyrdom and the suffering endured for faith
nearly nude body — vulnerability and Christ-like exposure to suffering
golden halo — sanctity and the crown of martyrdom
upward gaze — faith directed heavenward even in the midst of torment
landscape behind — the earthly world that the martyr transcends through suffering
description: A nearly nude young man stands upright, bound to what appears to be a tree or post, with several arrows piercing his torso and limbs. He wears only a white loincloth. A golden halo encircles his head. His body is muscular and idealized, his expression calm despite the arrows. A pastoral landscape with hills, buildings, and water stretches behind him under a pale sky. The figure stands on rocky ground.
theological_description: Saint Sebastian, a Roman soldier martyred during Diocletian's persecution (c. 288 AD), became one of the most depicted saints in Western art. Though a post-biblical figure, his martyrdom embodies the New Testament call to suffer for Christ. Paul's declaration, 'I bear on my body the marks of Jesus' (GAL.6.17), finds vivid expression in Sebastian's arrow-pierced flesh. His calm endurance reflects Romans 8:18: 'I consider that the sufferings of this present time are not worth comparing with the glory that is to be revealed.' The near-nudity of the figure parallels Christ's own stripping at the Crucifixion, while the upward gaze echoes Stephen, the first martyr, who 'gazed into heaven' at the moment of his death (ACT.7.55). Sebastian survived this initial attempt and was later beaten to death, making him a symbol of perseverance under persecution.

scripture_refs.reason: Bearing the marks of Jesus on the body — Sebastian's wounds as marks of faithful witness
Present sufferings not comparable to future glory — Sebastian's calm endurance
Stephen gazing into heaven at martyrdom, the pattern for Sebastian's upward gaze
Be faithful unto death, and I will give you the crown of life — the martyr's promise
